front: Poem: 'Beauty is truth, truth beauty' — addresses an 'Attic shape' called a 'foster-child of Silence and slow Time' and 'unravish'd bride of quietness'
Poem: the speaker desires to 'die upon the midnight with no pain'; asks 'Do I wake or sleep?' after declaring 'thou wast not born for death, immortal Bird!'
Poem: 'Much have I travell'd in the realms of gold'; feels like 'stout Cortez' staring at the Pacific or 'some watcher of the skies'
back: "On First Looking into Chapman's Homer" (John Keats)

front: Poem: 'O what can ail thee, knight-at-arms / Alone and palely loitering?' by a lake 'where the sedge has wither'd and no birds sing'
Poem: 'Season of mists and mellow fruitfulness'; 'Where are the songs of spring? Ay, where are they?'; ends with swallows twittering
Poem: unfinished epic about the fall of the Titans; abandoned because of 'too many Miltonic inversions'
Poem: opens 'A thing of beauty is a joy forever'; Hymn to Pan leads Wordsworth to call it 'a very pretty piece of paganism'
Author: letters contain the concept of negative capability: 'being in uncertainties, Mysteries, doubts, without any irritable reaching after fact and reason'
Author: letters describe life as a 'Mansion of Many Apartments' and the world as a 'vale of soul-making'
Poem: brothers discover Lorenzo's head in the title container; adapted from the Decameron
back: "Isabella; or the Pot of Basil" (John Keats)

front: Poem: Madeline falls asleep to see her future husband; Porphyro lays out 'candied apple, quince and plum and gourd'; nurse Angela and the Beadsman die
